item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Leaning on giving this an upvote, but for now I'll novote it while still parsing it. Manu Thirteen is easily the best part of the article, though.

item.title: Mod Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit was reverted by staff, as it introduced an error and also made unnecessary changes to ellipses in the work.

Matthew Nox, staff has already repeatedly addressed the issues with your problematic edits, specifically ellipses that did not need correction. Please ask in the discussion or contact the author if you wish to make edits from now on.

This is an official warning. Further infractions will result in a ban. You have been sent a wikidot message with this information.

Do not reply if you are not a staff member. This is a closed staff post.
Congrats! One of my favorite SCPs from the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's covered in the author post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article keeps referring to Irnini Mons, but the image is of Maat Mons. Just thought I'd point that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always enjoy Kalinin's work, and this is top-class. Manu-13 is my favorite character ever, and I wish that future setting wasn't such a hindrance so we could do more with him. I loved the imagery and the way the cognitohazard took old-fashioned spooks and not only made them work in the tone, but in the 2150 context. Plus an amazing version of the CotBG. So much to love here.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad to finally have time to read this massive chunk of writing. I am ambivalent about the future setting (since I prefer the Foundation-verse as urban fantasy set in "our world"1 than outright sci-fi in a hypothetical setting), but Manu-13 is a well-executed character.

Footnotes
1. Or at least a world that is similar to ours in terms of society, geopolitics and culture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really great. It is staggering in scope and utterly haunting. The presentation of the effects of the cognitohazard in the interviews is both subtle and horrific. Manu-13 is in its original appearance one of the best characters to come out of the wiki, ever.

This is the best SCP in the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I skipped this when it came out because long, but after reading that latest tale supplement, I had to know what I was dealing with. Plus, I mean, I missed out on the whole "this is happening way in the future" thing.

This is good, though the preponderance of tabs makes reading the interviews and uppy-downy scroll nightmare. That said, I honestly have no idea what I'm looking at, but this provides enough of a feeling to have been worth the read.

item.title: Re: Minor question
item.content:encoded: I don't usually use name generators for a variety of reasons, but in this case I did because I'm not terribly familiar with Korean naming customs. From your description, "Seung" sounds like it's an unusual but not unheard-of surname. If that's the case, I'm inclined to keep this as-is.

item.title: Minor question
item.content:encoded:
What's the real surname of the Lead Researcher?

Is it 'Seung', or 'Rim'? The surname Seung (승) is relatively rare surname in Korea (let me consider you used korean name to naming that lead researcher), while the surname Rim (임 or 림) is more common than Seung.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, not going for it. Its all to cryptic. Not really explaining what it does. I get that it takes their minds and they can see inside of it as the perspective as one of the aliens inside(?) but thats it. -1 for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I'll bite (as in, upvote).

For me, this falls dangerously close to the line between expungement being interesting and annoying.You certainly throw a lot of clues around, but for a lot of them it's hard to tell if you're hinting at a grander structure or using cheap parlor tricks (e.g., mandala imagery, seances, possession with bizarre language/behavior/medical consequences) to jazz up a boring idea.

It's not that I doubt that there is a neat idea behind it, it's just not clear to me from this article. Ultimately I'm left feeling like I've just finished the intriguing but indeterminate pilot to a TV series, and that is not a good feeling to have about a single article.

The things I did quite enjoy include the descriptions of what things were like inside the location, the Theta Prime reference (if intentional), Manu 13 as a character, and the conversation about it being sent along. Those were enough for an upvote, even with my reservations.

(The new red text redaction language gets an honorable mention, but I can't push the needle too high on that one because it is ultimately just another flavor of redaction, and that's kind of my central beef.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Both "skip" and "scip" have been used in tales - in the series involving UIU agents and AWCY?, whose overall title I don't recall, the Foundation is colloquially referred to by the agents as "Skippers".

And "clunky" is exactly what I would personally expect from people whose minds appear to have been at least partially subsumed by one or more alien consciousnesses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well now. This was an interesting article. Its not often that an article already has me drawn in by the end of the containment procedures, but you really nailed it. The Foundation infiltrating a massive colonization effort on Venus? Wow!

Additionally, I would have thought that an article set in the far future would be a little jarring, but thats not the case here. So kudos there as well.

If I was to gripe about anything, it would be that the interviews bog the article down a bit, mainly with their clunky dialogue. Granted, by I feel that they do their job well enough, and leave me asking numerous questions, but they are still a bit of a trudge to get through.

+1. This is a pretty solid set up for Rho-19.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Things I like about this-

  • Weird alien beings
  • Space
  • Scips that are explicitly set in the future
  • Most of the other stuff

Stuff I don't like-

  • The dialogue is clunky as fuck
  • "Skip" instead of "scip"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of old-school sci-fi, the paperbacks from the '50s that I read a lot of in middle school. This isn't what I'd expect from Kalinin, stylistically, but I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't think I didn't notice you using Theta Prime, there. Sounds like things are even worse than we think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Darn you for making me upvote a future-dated entry. X3
Now I have to headcanon this as salvaged from SCP-110 or something…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job, Kalinin. Really looking forward to the rest of the Rho-19 story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Knocking it out of the park once again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awesome job Kalinin. You combined space and cognitohazards into one article and pulled it off exceptionally well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The opening shot in the saga of Mobile Task Force Rho-19, "Cythereans." Thanks to my teammates, Von Pincier, minmin, and Zyn for review and for contributing to this venture.

Source image is free use courtesy of Wikipedia, and features the more photogenic Maat Mons. Original image may be accessed here. Image manipulation is my own work.

Rho-19 has its work cut out for it.
